<div><br></div>
<div><b>About</b></div>
<div><b>About</b></div>
<div>I help Enterprise SaaS companies scale; blending sales structure, partnership strategy, and customer success into one cohesive revenue engine that turns early traction into sustainable, predictable growth.</div>
<div><br></div>
<div>For over two decades IŐve led revenue and go-to-market teams across Israel, Europe, and North America. Over the years IŐve guided companies through transformation, from product-market fit to predictable scale. My strength lies in connecting worlds: between technology and business, strategy and execution, internal and partner teams.<br></div>
<div><br></div>
<div>At Gimmonix, I lead the commercial organization as CRO: uniting Sales, Success, and Support under one growth engine. In a single year we launched a new product, signed 30+ new enterprise accounts, reduced churn, and lifted NPS dramatically across the customer base. Along the way, weŐve embedded Agentic AI into daily workflows to accelerate decisions and connect teams.</div>
<div><br></div>
<div>Previously, I built BringgŐs global partner ecosystem from the ground up, turning partnerships into a true revenue driver that contributed 35% of company revenue. Earlier, at ClickSoftware, I helped shape the Salesforce alliance that redefined the market and eventually acquired by Salesforce for $1.35B.</div>
<div><br></div>
<div>What ties it all together is my belief that partnerships and ecosystems arenŐt a channel: theyŐre a philosophy. When product, people, and partners align around a shared purpose, growth compounds naturally.<br></div>
<div><br></div>
<div>IŐm passionate about helping SaaS scale-ups reach that next level: where collaboration replaces silos, customer value drives revenue, and every connection in the ecosystem becomes part of the growth story.<br></div>
<div><br></div>
<div>Always open to connecting with founders, investors, and executive teams navigating their next stage of scale. Especially where product, ecosystem, and AI meet.<br></div>
<div><br></div>
<div>Specialties: Revenue Leadership, Chief Revenue Officer, CRO, VP Sales, GTM Strategy, Go-to-Market, Partnerships, Alliances, Ecosystem GTM, Enterprise SaaS, B2B SaaS, Travel Tech, Logistics Tech, Salesforce Partnerships, Channel Sales, Indirect Sales, Partner Programs, Customer Success, Enterprise Sales, Sales Operations, AI GTM, Agentic AI, Revenue Intelligence, Product-Led Growth, Market Expansion, New Market Entry, Strategic Partnerships, SaaS Scalability, Pipeline Management, Executive Leadership.<br></div>
<div>I help Enterprise SaaS companies scale; blending sales structure, partnership strategy, and customer success into one cohesive revenue engine that turns early traction into sustainable, predictable growth. For over two decades IŐve led revenue and go-to-market teams across Israel, Europe, and North America. Over the years IŐve guided companies through transformation, from product-market fit to predictable scale. My strength lies in connecting worlds: between technology and business, strategy and execution, internal and partner teams. At Gimmonix, I lead the commercial organization as CRO: uniting Sales, Success, and Support under one growth engine. In a single year we launched a new product, signed 30+ new enterprise accounts, reduced churn, and lifted NPS dramatically across the customer base. Along the way, weŐve embedded Agentic AI into daily workflows to accelerate decisions and connect teams. Previously, I built BringgŐs global partner ecosystem from the ground up, turning partnerships into a true revenue driver that contributed 35% of company revenue. Earlier, at ClickSoftware, I helped shape the Salesforce alliance that redefined the market and eventually acquired by Salesforce for $1.35B. What ties it all together is my belief that partnerships and ecosystems arenŐt a channel: theyŐre a philosophy. When product, people, and partners align around a shared purpose, growth compounds naturally. IŐm passionate about helping SaaS scale-ups reach that next level: where collaboration replaces silos, customer value drives revenue, and every connection in the ecosystem becomes part of the growth story. Always open to connecting with founders, investors, and executive teams navigating their next stage of scale. Especially where product, ecosystem, and AI meet. Specialties: Revenue Leadership, Chief Revenue Officer, CRO, VP Sales, GTM Strategy, Go-to-Market, Partnerships, Alliances, Ecosystem GTM, Enterprise SaaS, B2B SaaS, Travel Tech, Logistics Tech, Salesforce Partnerships, Channel Sales, Indirect Sales, Partner Programs, Customer Success, Enterprise Sales, Sales Operations, AI GTM, Agentic AI, Revenue Intelligence, Product-Led Growth, Market Expansion, New Market Entry, Strategic Partnerships, SaaS Scalability, Pipeline Management, Executive Leadership.<br></div>
<div>Ésee more</div>
<ul>
<li><br><b>Top skillsTop skills</b>Go-to-Market Strategy Ą Sales Ą Strategic Partnerships Ą Software as a Service (SaaS) Ą agentic AIGo-to-Market Strategy Ą Sales Ą Strategic Partnerships Ą Software as a Service (SaaS) Ą agentic AI<b><br></b><br></li>
</ul>
<div><br></div>
<div><b>Featured</b></div>
<div><b>Featured</b></div>
<ul>
<li>LinkLink<b><br></b><b><br></b><br></li>
<li><b><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/jpeg;base64,"/><br></b><b><br></b></li>
<li><b>ALLIED FORCES: Partnerships And Alliances For Joint Customer Value, Part 1ALLIED FORCES: Partnerships And Alliances For Joint Customer Value, Part 1</b>YouTubeYouTube<b><br></b>A customer wants to solve a business problem. That does not start or end with a single piece of technology; it should incorporate a holistic approach to de-risking the project and deliver assured success.A customer wants to solve a business problem. That does not start or end with a single piece of technology; it should incorporate a holistic approach to de-risking the project and deliver assured success.<br></li>
<li>ArticleArticle<b><br></b><b><br></b><br></li>
<li><b><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/png;base64,"/><br></b><b><br></b></li>
<li><b>7 Years at Click, 3 Key Lessons Learnt7 Years at Click, 3 Key Lessons Learnt</b>Nikolai Avrutov-Rostarchook on LinkedInNikolai Avrutov-Rostarchook on LinkedIn<b><br></b>Seven years ago I joined Click, impressed by its history, market leadership, mission critical technology, and the sheer talent of its people. This was a time of tremendous professional growth as we transformed not only the company itself, but also the entire industry. As I move on to my next great challenge, these are my reflections on key lessons learnt. Build Trust Click is packed with deep domain expertise, years of experience and amazing technology. Working out of the R&ampD hub here in Israel was an empowering learning opportunity. From day one I was challenged with advancing ClickŐs latest technologies, from the first iterations in the cloud, to our Salesforce-related solutions. A lot of it was new, unproven and risky. In order to succeed, our executive sponsor, my team and I needed internal and external stakeholders to invest, take an active part in our plans, and sometimes even put their careers at risk, in other words to trust us. We earned it through hard work backed by deep knowledge (and continuous learning), meticulous follow-through on commitments, transparent communication, sheer enthusiasm, and heartfelt inclusion in our vision of what the future might look like. Our first step with Salesforce was a grassroots initiative, when a small team of ÔintrapreneursŐ across sales and engineering came together to build our very first app - ShiftExpert. In less than six months we had a ready to go product, and we were all-over our first customer: an NGO based out of New Zealand. All hands on deck were committed to make sure this customer was a success - and it was, establishing along the way a great deal of trust within the Team, with the customer and within the organization. Sales poured in, and we were provided with a mandate to build our next app - a full blown field service solution, native on Salesforce. Trust was (and is) an essential foundation for any collaborative endeavour without which we couldnŐt reach the scale we are at today. Stand on the bridge between Business &amp Engineering We are in the business of mission-critical technology. Driving new initiatives at Click was a multi-disciplinary act. A key for my teamŐs success was to learn how to combine sales acumen with technical expertise on one hand, and connect all teams to the market on the other. DonŐt get me wrong, I never wrote a single line of code but fully understand how things are built. I was extremely hands on, able to self configure, self demo, lead a competent discussion with both business and IT stakeholders and even educate on the transformational implications of leveraging our solutions. In many situations I found myself serving as a bridge between the technical and business worlds, and so did many members of my team. This was absolutely crucial as we sought to build momentum, mobilize R&ampD and business development initiatives to propel our technology far and wide. I recall conducting regular meetings with the engineering team, bringing back stories from the field about exciting sales cycles, unusual use cases, and relationship breakthroughs adding a new dimension of inclusion and appreciation of the impact their hard work has on the world. Many of our engineers brought their sales engineering background from before. The mix resulted in market and sales oriented development, and the Team was excited to deliver innovative features and capabilities that were demanded by customers and helped to rapidly achieve results in the field. Inspire, Educate, Mobilize Six of my seven years at Click focused on building our amazing alliance with Salesforce. In the early days it was Yoav Kolodner and Uri Pintov who envisioned the (im)possible, and Udi Keidar our executive sponsor who paved the way forward. Then the rapid growth of our ISV practice, evolving to the sheer scale of the OEM relationship eventually culminating with the acquisition of Click by Salesforce. All along, an essential ingredient for success was the people who - as Mark Cattini once described it - Ňdrove the curveÓ. A lot of people played various critical roles over the years and to recruit these people we needed to evangelise our vision: Be vocal about whatŐs exciting Everyone is happy to join a successful ride, but before success is proven - people need to see the light, and understand whatŐs so exciting about the future. We used several platforms to communicate our vision and the path to success. Without the buy-in, building momentum wouldŐve been much more difficult. Something as simple as a LinkedIn post describing an on site activity, with the right hashtag in place - went a long way to update the growing community on what is going on. Scale with a diverse team We assembled an uber talented team of domain experts, developers, strategists, digital transformation consultants, and success engineers - all weighing in at the right place at the right time. Gradually our initiatives evolved into structured programmes (partner training courses, sales bootcamps, on site customer success, etc) but our operation mode was of continuous iteration, feedback and collaboration. A true team-play approach. This was a key ingredient to the overall success of the project. Provide tools, framework and solutions to what is hard A rosy image of the future is fine, but smart people look at the obstacles, challenges, and what could go wrong on the way to glory. Aware of this, we built models, created tools and frameworks to articulate the challenges and paths to overcome them. We translated these into programmized education tracks tailored for pre sales, sales, marketing, implementation, support and success teams lasting anywhere between 45 minutes to several days (depending on who we trained, and how much time we were given). To execute these programmes we embarked on a glorious world tour (yours truly took 50+ trips worldwide, and as a team we travelled well over 3M Miles in the last three years alone!). Our ground activities were complemented by online (live as well as on demand) resources. Amplify your message I learnt that inclusion is an important part of building the much-sought-after momentum. And so, we made it a point to communicate and reverberate many of the milestones and activities we performed globally. LinkedIn, Chatter groups, internal newsletters, and of course personal communications - all established a sense of inclusion and continuity. This in turn formed a global community around our initiatives, generated excitement, further trust, and greatly contributed to the growth momentum of our project. My years at Click were a time of great change, innovation and transformation. At the heart of it were people with great love and passion for our company and industry, the courage to think outside the box and execute on a great vision. Trust was the foundation. It made room for radical innovation, fuelled excitement, and enabled people to take calculated risks. A diverse group of talented people successfully incubated great ideas and rolled them out to the market. Relentless commitment to success coupled with pragmatic approach and transparency helped to build rapid momentum and led to exponential growth. We built on solid foundations of domain expertise and leading technology, dared to challenge the common wisdom and pivot in a new and exciting direction resulting in a world class product, close-knit community, and dare I say brought about a transformational impact on our industry!Seven years ago I joined Click, impressed by its history, market leadership, mission critical technology, and the sheer talent of its people. This was a time of tremendous professional growth as we transformed not only the company itself, but also the entire industry. As I move on to my next great challenge, these are my reflections on key lessons learnt. Build Trust Click is packed with deep domain expertise, years of experience and amazing technology. Working out of the R&ampD hub here in Israel was an empowering learning opportunity. From day one I was challenged with advancing ClickŐs latest technologies, from the first iterations in the cloud, to our Salesforce-related solutions. A lot of it was new, unproven and risky. In order to succeed, our executive sponsor, my team and I needed internal and external stakeholders to invest, take an active part in our plans, and sometimes even put their careers at risk, in other words to trust us. We earned it through hard work backed by deep knowledge (and continuous learning), meticulous follow-through on commitments, transparent communication, sheer enthusiasm, and heartfelt inclusion in our vision of what the future might look like. Our first step with Salesforce was a grassroots initiative, when a small team of ÔintrapreneursŐ across sales and engineering came together to build our very first app - ShiftExpert. In less than six months we had a ready to go product, and we were all-over our first customer: an NGO based out of New Zealand. All hands on deck were committed to make sure this customer was a success - and it was, establishing along the way a great deal of trust within the Team, with the customer and within the organization. Sales poured in, and we were provided with a mandate to build our next app - a full blown field service solution, native on Salesforce. Trust was (and is) an essential foundation for any collaborative endeavour without which we couldnŐt reach the scale we are at today. Stand on the bridge between Business &amp Engineering We are in the business of mission-critical technology. Driving new initiatives at Click was a multi-disciplinary act. A key for my teamŐs success was to learn how to combine sales acumen with technical expertise on one hand, and connect all teams to the market on the other. DonŐt get me wrong, I never wrote a single line of code but fully understand how things are built. I was extremely hands on, able to self configure, self demo, lead a competent discussion with both business and IT stakeholders and even educate on the transformational implications of leveraging our solutions. In many situations I found myself serving as a bridge between the technical and business worlds, and so did many members of my team. This was absolutely crucial as we sought to build momentum, mobilize R&ampD and business development initiatives to propel our technology far and wide. I recall conducting regular meetings with the engineering team, bringing back stories from the field about exciting sales cycles, unusual use cases, and relationship breakthroughs adding a new dimension of inclusion and appreciation of the impact their hard work has on the world. Many of our engineers brought their sales engineering background from before. The mix resulted in market and sales oriented development, and the Team was excited to deliver innovative features and capabilities that were demanded by customers and helped to rapidly achieve results in the field. Inspire, Educate, Mobilize Six of my seven years at Click focused on building our amazing alliance with Salesforce. In the early days it was Yoav Kolodner and Uri Pintov who envisioned the (im)possible, and Udi Keidar our executive sponsor who paved the way forward. Then the rapid growth of our ISV practice, evolving to the sheer scale of the OEM relationship eventually culminating with the acquisition of Click by Salesforce. All along, an essential ingredient for success was the people who - as Mark Cattini once described it - Ňdrove the curveÓ. A lot of people played various critical roles over the years and to recruit these people we needed to evangelise our vision: Be vocal about whatŐs exciting Everyone is happy to join a successful ride, but before success is proven - people need to see the light, and understand whatŐs so exciting about the future. We used several platforms to communicate our vision and the path to success. Without the buy-in, building momentum wouldŐve been much more difficult. Something as simple as a LinkedIn post describing an on site activity, with the right hashtag in place - went a long way to update the growing community on what is going on. Scale with a diverse team We assembled an uber talented team of domain experts, developers, strategists, digital transformation consultants, and success engineers - all weighing in at the right place at the right time. Gradually our initiatives evolved into structured programmes (partner training courses, sales bootcamps, on site customer success, etc) but our operation mode was of continuous iteration, feedback and collaboration. A true team-play approach. This was a key ingredient to the overall success of the project. Provide tools, framework and solutions to what is hard A rosy image of the future is fine, but smart people look at the obstacles, challenges, and what could go wrong on the way to glory. Aware of this, we built models, created tools and frameworks to articulate the challenges and paths to overcome them. We translated these into programmized education tracks tailored for pre sales, sales, marketing, implementation, support and success teams lasting anywhere between 45 minutes to several days (depending on who we trained, and how much time we were given). To execute these programmes we embarked on a glorious world tour (yours truly took 50+ trips worldwide, and as a team we travelled well over 3M Miles in the last three years alone!). Our ground activities were complemented by online (live as well as on demand) resources. Amplify your message I learnt that inclusion is an important part of building the much-sought-after momentum. And so, we made it a point to communicate and reverberate many of the milestones and activities we performed globally. LinkedIn, Chatter groups, internal newsletters, and of course personal communications - all established a sense of inclusion and continuity. This in turn formed a global community around our initiatives, generated excitement, further trust, and greatly contributed to the growth momentum of our project. My years at Click were a time of great change, innovation and transformation. At the heart of it were people with great love and passion for our company and industry, the courage to think outside the box and execute on a great vision. Trust was the foundation. It made room for radical innovation, fuelled excitement, and enabled people to take calculated risks. A diverse group of talented people successfully incubated great ideas and rolled them out to the market. Relentless commitment to success coupled with pragmatic approach and transparency helped to build rapid momentum and led to exponential growth. We built on solid foundations of domain expertise and leading technology, dared to challenge the common wisdom and pivot in a new and exciting direction resulting in a world class product, close-knit community, and dare I say brought about a transformational impact on our industry!<br></li>
</ul>
<div><b><br></b></div>
<div><b><br></b></div>
<div><b><br></b></div>
<div><b><br></b></div>
<div><br></div>
<div><br></div>
<div><b>Activity</b></div>
<div><b>Activity</b></div>
<div>5,978 followers</div>
<div>5,978 followers<br></div>
<div><b><br></b></div>
<div><b>Following</b></div>
<div><b>Posts</b><b><br></b></div>
<div><b>Comments</b><b><br></b></div>
<div><b>Images</b></div>
<div>Loaded 3 Posts posts</div>
<ul>
<li><b>Nikolai Avrutov-Rostarchook</b> posted this Ą 6d6dOver the past two years IŐve been experimenting a lot with AI.ĘThe vison was grand. Like many people, GenAI pulled me in. Since then I tried dozens of tools (some of them were dramatic game changers!), built workflows, tested automations, watched agents emerge, and explored what this could mean for Revenue Engineering. Some things worked surprisingly well. Many things didnŐt. What became clear very quickly is that the hardest part is not the technology. The hardest part is adoption inside a real organization. -&gt Where do you start? -&gt Which processes actually move KPIs? -&gt How do you avoid months of experimentation that lead nowhere? -&gt What Software and business processes can I actually replace and enhance? At several points during that journey I caught myself thinking: I wish I had someone experienced sitting next to me helping figure this out. Someone who could help identify the right starting points, reduce trial and error, and focus the work on business impact rather than tools. Someone like Michael Weinberg and Andrey Aparinov. That idea eventually turned into something real. I recently became a Partner at Resonance Team. Together we are a group of experienced operators who are helping companies adopt AI in a practical, KPI-driven way. Not from a purely technical perspective. But from an executive and operational lens. Starting with the business metrics that matter, and then working backwards to the technology that can actually move them. More to share soon. In the meantime IŐm curious: Where is AI already helping your team move real business metrics? </li>
<li><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/jpeg;base64,"/><br></li>
<li><br><br>Over the past two years IŐve been experimenting a lot with AI.ĘThe vison was grand.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Like many people, GenAI pulled me in. Since then I tried dozens of tools (some of them were dramatic game changers!), built workflows, tested automations, watched agents emerge, and explored what this could mean for Revenue Engineering.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Some things worked surprisingly well.</li>
<li>Many things didnŐt.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>What became clear very quickly is that the hardest part is not the technology. The hardest part is adoption inside a real organization.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>-&gt Where do you start?</li>
<li>-&gt Which processes actually move KPIs?</li>
<li>-&gt How do you avoid months of experimentation that lead nowhere?</li>
<li>-&gt What Software and business processes can I actually replace and enhance?</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>At several points during that journey I caught myself thinking:</li>
<li>I wish I had someone experienced sitting next to me helping figure this out.</li>
<li>Someone who could help identify the right starting points, reduce trial and error, and focus the work on business impact rather than tools. Someone like <b>Michael Weinberg</b> and <b>Andrey Aparinov</b>.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>That idea eventually turned into something real.</li>
<li>I recently became a Partner at <b>Resonance Team</b>.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Together we are a group of experienced operators who are helping companies adopt AI in a practical, KPI-driven way.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Not from a purely technical perspective.</li>
<li>But from an executive and operational lens.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Starting with the business metrics that matter, and then working backwards to the technology that can actually move them.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>More to share soon.</li>
<li>In the meantime IŐm curious:</li>
<li>Where is AI already helping your team move real business metrics?</li>
<ul>
<li>Éshow more <br></li>
<li><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/><br></li>
<li><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/><br></li>
<li><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/><br></li>
</ul>
<li>29 5 comments </li>
<li><b>Nikolai Avrutov-Rostarchook</b> posted this Ą 1w1wWe were told automation equals progress. For a while, it looked that way. New workflow. New AI layer. More velocity. Then a field changes. A dependency is undocumented. A sync fails silently. And suddenly it is not a technical glitch. It is a trust problem. From the CRO seat, I have seen this too many times: Forecasts wobble. Attribution becomes Ňgood enough.Ó RevOps and IT start debugging instead of building. Automation did not fail. Architecture did. Automation without architectural context does not create progress. It accelerates entropy. Speed hides cracks. Scale amplifies them. At scale, governance cannot sit outside delivery as a review meeting or a PDF policy. It has to live inside the system. Embedded. Continuous. Every automated action must understand the metadata it touches. Every change must know its downstream impact. Progress is not how many flows you launch. It is how many you still trust six months later. If you are layering AI on top of brittle foundations, pause. The real unlock is not more automation. It is intelligent, system aware automation. If that tension feels familiar, we should compare notes. If youŐre heading to #TDX26, letŐs grab a coffee and compare notes. </li>
<li><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/jpeg;base64,"/><br></li>
<li><br><br>We were told automation equals progress.</li>
<li>For a while, it looked that way.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>New workflow.</li>
<li>New AI layer.</li>
<li>More velocity.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Then a field changes.</li>
<li>A dependency is undocumented.</li>
<li>A sync fails silently.</li>
<li>And suddenly it is not a technical glitch.</li>
<li>It is a trust problem.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>From the CRO seat, I have seen this too many times:</li>
<li>Forecasts wobble.</li>
<li>Attribution becomes Ňgood enough.Ó</li>
<li>RevOps and IT start debugging instead of building.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Automation did not fail.</li>
<li>Architecture did.</li>
<li>Automation without architectural context does not create progress. It accelerates entropy.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Speed hides cracks.</li>
<li>Scale amplifies them.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>At scale, governance cannot sit outside delivery as a review meeting or a PDF policy. It has to live inside the system. Embedded. Continuous.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Every automated action must understand the metadata it touches. Every change must know its downstream impact.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Progress is not how many flows you launch.</li>
<li>It is how many you still trust six months later.</li>
<li>If you are layering AI on top of brittle foundations, pause.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>The real unlock is not more automation.</li>
<li>It is intelligent, system aware automation.</li>
<li>If that tension feels familiar, we should compare notes.</li>
<li><br></li>
<ul>
<li>If youŐre heading to <b>hashtag#TDX26</b>, letŐs grab a coffee and compare notes. Éshow more <br></li>
<li><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/><br></li>
<li><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/><br></li>
</ul>
<li>26 </li>
<li><b>Nikolai Avrutov-Rostarchook</b> posted this Ą 3w3wWhen business results stall, the instinct is predictable - do more: Add contractors. Add capacity. Add cost. IŐve seen companies increase delivery budgets by millions and still watch revenue initiatives slip, strategic projects stall, and operating costs quietly compound. Because the real bottleneck was never headcount. It was clarity. When no one has a complete, trusted view of how systems, logic, and dependencies actually connect into a coherent business process, execution becomes fragile. Every change requires interpretation. Every initiative moves slower than planned. Every outcome becomes harder, more expensive, and less predictable to achieve. The cost isnŐt just slower delivery. ItŐs delayed revenue. Missed market timing. Lower return on every dollar invested in technology and operations. Adding people doesnŐt resolve this. It increases coordination overhead. It spreads fragmented understanding across more individuals. And costs rise faster than business throughput. This is why some organizations invest heavily and still struggle to translate effort into results. The shift happens when the system itself becomes understandable. When context is visible. When dependencies are explicit. When tribal knowledge becomes the system, Throughput increases. Costs stabilize. Business outcomes accelerate. Not because more people were added. But because the intelligence replaced guesswork. This is where the highest leverage exists. And itŐs the work I focus on every day. </li>
<li><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/jpeg;base64,"/><br></li>
<li><br><br>When business results stall, the instinct is predictable - do more:</li>
<li>Add contractors.</li>
<li>Add capacity.</li>
<li>Add cost.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>IŐve seen companies increase delivery budgets by millions and still watch revenue initiatives slip, strategic projects stall, and operating costs quietly compound.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Because the real bottleneck was never headcount.</li>
<li>It was clarity.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>When no one has a complete, trusted view of how systems, logic, and dependencies actually connect into a coherent business process, execution becomes fragile.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Every change requires interpretation.</li>
<li>Every initiative moves slower than planned.</li>
<li>Every outcome becomes harder, more expensive, and less predictable to achieve.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>The cost isnŐt just slower delivery.</li>
<li>ItŐs delayed revenue.</li>
<li>Missed market timing.</li>
<li>Lower return on every dollar invested in technology and operations.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Adding people doesnŐt resolve this.</li>
<li>It increases coordination overhead.</li>
<li>It spreads fragmented understanding across more individuals.</li>
<li>And costs rise faster than business throughput.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>This is why some organizations invest heavily and still struggle to translate effort into results.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>The shift happens when the system itself becomes understandable.</li>
<li>When context is visible.</li>
<li>When dependencies are explicit.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>When tribal knowledge becomes the system,</li>
<li>Throughput increases.</li>
<li>Costs stabilize.</li>
<li>Business outcomes accelerate.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Not because more people were added.</li>
<li>But because the intelligence replaced guesswork.</li>
<li>This is where the highest leverage exists.</li>
<li><br></li>
<ul>
<li>And itŐs the work I focus on every day. Éshow more <br></li>
<li><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/><br></li>
<li><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/><br></li>
<li><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,"/><br></li>
</ul>
<li>28 4 comments </li>
</ul>

<ul>
<ul>
<li>Partner at Resonance, a consulting firm helping companies turn AI from buzzword into operational advantage. We work with leadership teams to identify where AI can meaningfully improve revenue, operations, and decision making, then build and deploy production systems that deliver measurable results. Our approach is pragmatic and execution-focused: start with real business problems, prove value quickly, and scale from there. After years operating inside technology companies across sales, product, and operations, we bring both strategic perspective and hands-on execution to help organizations adopt AI in ways that actually work.Partner at Resonance, a consulting firm helping companies turn AI from buzzword into operational advantage. <li><b><br></b><br><b>Chief Revenue Officer (CRO)Chief Revenue Officer (CRO)</b>Gimmonix á Full-timeGimmonix á Full-timeNov 2024 - Nov 2025 á 1 yr 1 moNov 2024 to Nov 2025 á 1 yr 1 moTel Aviv District, Israel á HybridTel Aviv District, Israel á Hybrid<br></li>
<ul>
<ul>
<li>Gimmonix is a global SaaS provider for travel and hospitality connectivity, serving 100+ enterprise customers across EMEA, APAC &amp the Americas, in collaboration with 200+ partners.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>As the GTM Leader at GimmonixŐs I am leading the global commercial organization Ń Sales, Success, and Support Ń guiding the companyŐs transition from project-based delivery to a scalable, data-driven SaaS model.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>- Leading global Enterprise SaaS GTM (Sales, CS, Support) across EMEA/AMER/APAC</li>
<li>- Full P&ampL ownership, doubled ARR, reduced churn by 80%</li>
<li>- Built scalable GTM operating system, pipeline governance, and forecasting discipline</li>
<li>- Drove AI/Agentic AI commercialization and productivity programs</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>In my first year, we launched RateFox, our new revenue intelligence product, created substantial pipleine resulting in 30+ new accounts won, dramatically reduced churn and achieved record customer satisfaction level, while embedding Agentic AI into our workflows to drive smarter, faster decisions.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>My focus: SaaS Revenue growth by building operational balance between pipeline growth and sales execution, aligning product, customer, and partner success under one cohesive, scalable GTM and predictable revenue engine.Gimmonix is a global SaaS provider for travel and hospitality connectivity, serving 100+ enterprise customers across EMEA, APAC &amp the Americas, in collaboration with 200+ partners. <li><b><br></b><br><b>FounderFounder</b>Gleam Parenting á Full-timeGleam Parenting á Full-timeSep 2023 - Oct 2024 á 1 yr 2 mosSep 2023 to Oct 2024 á 1 yr 2 mos<br></li>
<ul>
<ul>
<li>I founded Gleam to solve a family pain: the endless battle over screen time. </li>
<li><br></li>
<li>The concept is simple: make kids earn their screen time, by completing offline chores and online learning. A digital version of 'chores first play later'. </li>
<li><br></li>
<li>First production versions were published in Google and Apple playstores in 2024. </li>
<li><br></li>
<li>The project is paused: </li>
<li>While the concept proved as working (with my kids) and gained initial traction with ±100 families, it wasn't sufficient to justify the needed investment in UX&ampI to enable broader adoption.I founded Gleam to solve a family pain: the endless battle over screen time. The concept is simple: make kids earn their screen time, by completing offline chores and online learning. A digital version of 'chores first play later'. First production versions were published in Google and Apple playstores in 2024. The project is paused: While the concept proved as working (with my kids) and gained initial traction with ±100 families, it wasn't sufficient to justify the needed investment in UX&ampI to enable broader adoption.Ésee more </li>
</ul>

<div><b>Recommendations</b></div>
<div><b>Recommendations</b></div>
<div><b>Recommend Nikolai</b></div>
<div><b>Received</b><b><br></b></div>
<div><b>Received</b><b><br></b></div>
<div><b>Given</b></div>
<div><b>Given</b><b><br></b></div>

<li><b><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/jpeg;base64,"/><br></b><b><br></b></li>
<li><b><br></b><br><b>Andrey AparinovAndrey Aparinov</b>á 2ndSecond degree connectionPartner | AI Consulting &amp Product Development | Mid-Market AI Systems | Travel á 3PL á Real EstatePartner | AI Consulting &amp Product Development | Mid-Market AI Systems | Travel á 3PL á Real EstateOctober 19, 2025, Andrey worked with Nikolai on the same teamOctober 19, 2025, Andrey worked with Nikolai on the same team<br></li>
<ul>
<ul>
<li>Late in 2024 we both came to travel tech from other industries not only to maintain existing solutions and customer base but to push a new product (Ratefox) to market. As members of the executive team, I owned the production and development domain and Nikolai held all client relationships.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>A fast learner, hands-on and real team player, he is the best person I've ever seen to build trust with clients and build a team that really cares, all with a far reaching strategic perspective, as a colleague on the executive team.</li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Nikolai and his team were able to highlight the strong sides of our product and mitigate production drawbacks, forming it into an evolving process and building an effective and predictable relationship with our clients. </li>
<li><br></li>
<li>Notably, NikolaiŐs team was the first to introduce fully functioning AI agents to day-to-day support and success team activities, chart and evolve the sales and onboarding journeys and lay initial foundations to effective future growth.Late in 2024 we both came to travel tech from other industries not only to maintain existing solutions and customer base but to push a new product (Ratefox) to market. As members of the executive team, I owned the production and development domain and Nikolai held all client relationships. A fast learner, hands-on and real team player, he is the best person I've ever seen to build trust with clients and build a team that really cares, all with a far reaching strategic perspective, as a colleague on the executive team. Nikolai and his team were able to highlight the strong sides of our product and mitigate production drawbacks, forming it into an evolving process and building an effective and predictable relationship with our clients. Notably, NikolaiŐs team was the first to introduce fully functioning AI agents to day-to-day support and success team activities, chart and evolve the sales and onboarding journeys and lay initial foundations to effective future growth.Ésee more </li>
</ul>
</ul>
<li><br></li>
<li><br></li>
<li><b><img style="max-width: 100%; max-height: 100%;" src="data:image/jpeg;base64,"/><br></b><b><br></b></li>
<li><b><br></b><br><b>Steven EngelenSteven Engelen</b>á 2ndSecond degree connectionSalesforce Field Service consultancy (freelance). Driving product and customer success by combining best in practice business processes and best in class IT solutions.Salesforce Field Service consultancy (freelance). Driving product and customer success by combining best in practice business processes and best in class IT solutions.April 17, 2023, Steven worked with Nikolai but they were at different companiesApril 17, 2023, Steven worked with Nikolai but they were at different companies<br></li>
<ul>
<ul>
<li>I met Nikolai when Salesforce started to roll out Field Service on the Salesforce platform and we quickly became friends. Nikolai was very open and willing to share his extensive product knowledge, and thanks to his enthusiasm and customer focus we could quickly grow the field service market for Salesforce. I would even risk saying that the Field Service market would have been very different without Nikolai, as he was able to bring the product (and its customers) to a whole new level of experience. Nikolai has a very Ňyes you canÓ mentality, and in my opinion is someone that will have a very high impact on your organisation and the way you operate.I met Nikolai when Salesforce started to roll out Field Service on the Salesforce platform and we quickly became friends. Nikolai was very open and willing to share his extensive product knowledge, and thanks to his enthusiasm and customer focus we could quickly grow the field service market for Salesforce. I would even risk saying that the Field Service market would have been very different without Nikolai, as he was able to bring the product (and its customers) to a whole new level of experience. Nikolai has a very Ňyes you canÓ mentality, and in my opinion is someone that will have a very high impact on your organisation and the way you operate.</li>
</ul>
</ul>

<li><br></li>
</ul>
